text: This meeting is being recorded.
I'm doing well.
How about yourself, Tyler?
Oh, man.
Always feeling good on a Friday.
Are you calling in from Atlanta?
How's things in Atlanta?
Much better than they have been in the past.
It's been pretty cold for some odd reason here in November, but it's worn back up.
It's, you know, back in like the 60s, close to 70s, so always a beautiful day when you get some warmth and you can wear a short sleeve t-shirt.
For sure.
I'm in Houston, so we got the same kind of weather.
I totally get it.
I And yeah.
And it's definitely weird when it's 80, you know, in like mid-November, like, oh, wait, I might be sweating a little bit, but, you know, we live here for a reason, right?
Yeah, it's nice to not have to deal with the snow and needing snow tires.
So, you know, save a little money that way, too.
For sure.
Well, I'm excited to chat with you today here.
For context, I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX and I've been here for about five months.
And it's really unique in terms of my sales experience.
Like, GrowthX is very different.
You know, we're like selling services, but we're building software.
And the issue is not, you know, can we close the deals?
It's, it's, we find the scalability to like, to be able to work with everybody that wants to work with us.
So I'm excited about the product launch and about this new role because we're about to get to a place where we can work with a lot more clients and it should just open up the floodgates for sales.

So, yeah, I've got some questions that I got to ask you, but to start off, like, I really just want to learn more about you and, you know, your story and sales and see that you're like, you know, own your own business right now.
So I would love to hear a little bit more about that, the consulting services, and then we can kind of go from there.
But, yeah, just help me get to know you a little bit better to start off.
Yeah, been in sales a while.
What, going on 13 years here soon?
Started outside sales.
did phones, POS, merchant services, structure, settlements, insurance, sold cars.
Pretty much did it all before I got into tech about nine years ago.
Started at a value at a reseller, actually.
So hardware, software, and services.
We did a mix of everything.
Doing kind of the MSB portion as well.
We spent about a year there before moving on to Foxit.
I spent about four years, second BDR hire, rebuild all the processes, pretty much found all the tools, got that Excel spreadsheet finally, and hired a lot of the team.
And so from that, I was promoted, worked as an AE, exceeded, and was number one on my team when it came to quote on revenue attainment every year, 1.6, 1.8, 2.8 million on the last year.
And then I moved on to Tulio, so much larger company.
Unfortunately, that team was cut short due to the large layoff that Tulio had.
But during my time, I did find some success.
You know, I had some quarters over quota as well in the few quarters that I did have under tenure.
And from there, I moved on to Contentful, where once again, I hit the ground running, number one A in North America, Q1, half one as well.
From there, I was brought on to the Southeast territory to help grow it due to some of the turnover I was experiencing.
And so I grew that territory 20% before experiencing what was the third layoff in my career.
From there, you know, I did find.
I my own company, you know, which is part of the reason why I took the role at Cypress.
was actually a contract role, which I was helping to build a lot of processes there for Outbound.
Also doing some sales as well before Q1, both in your 200%.
And then also doing some consulting, you know, for family businesses and things like that.
So just more on the sales portion of how that works.
And so now that contract's ended, you know, I'm looking for the next role, next opportunity.
Hopefully something that's long-term as well.
Something like at FOXA tenure and, you know, Contentful was about two years as well.
But it's something where I can see myself growing, being an impact player as well.
And, you hopefully taking the skills that I've learned throughout all these years of sales and applying them somewhere else.
Love it.
Yeah, very, like, perfectly succinct, well-articulated.
Would you continue doing, like, the consulting services if you were also doing the role full-time at GrowthX?
Or what are you thinking in terms of that?
Uh, no.
I want to do something a bit different.
Um.
I'd still be helping out family and stuff with their businesses, and that's something that's kind of separate from the whole consulting services.
More so what I wanted to do mainly with that was hopefully build something for sellers in general.

It's not supposed to be something that's necessarily revenue generating, but a way to bring sellers together, I think, in SaaS and technology in general and a where we can all grow and improve together, share tips, things like that.
But that's something that's for the future.
Right now I'm focused on more of what I'm doing.
It's super cool, and just to dig in a little bit more, the consulting services, what part of the sales process do you feel like you're specializing in with some of the work that you're doing there?

A lot of it is, one, kind of like, let's say, optimizing a of the processes, kind of cutting down on the time, making sure that when it comes to things like discovery, the right questions are being asked, how you handle follow-ups as well.
You know, I talk to a lot of people who don't have sales experience, so they're not really sure what they should be doing.
And so a lot of it is kind of teaching, you what they should be doing, you know, how they should be working with and handling customers, you how to price certain things as well.
Just various pieces of it that people need help with.
I see what you mean.
What, like, methodology do you particularly like when it comes to sales?
I'm big on value selling.
I mean, I use MedPick and Medic for my framework.
I don't like to say it's a methodology.
I think it's more of a framework, just making sure that you're gathering the right information, you know, that it's a solid opportunity.
I think it's great for that.

But when it comes to actually going through a sales process, I think value and solution selling are very key, and it really hits home on figuring out, you know, business objectives and what's the overall outcome of purchasing this new software?
How does it affect the business as a whole?
A hundred percent.
I mean, you got to like quantify value, you got to connect to value.
you.
you.
you.
Thank

But you also have to just like earn someone's trust, which I don't think, you know, I don't think like thinking framework and methodology oriented for just like the conversation, the human part of it is good, right?
Like if you get too framework oriented, I feel like it can kind of ignore something that is like core to building a champion, you know what I mean?
Um, but love that you have, you know, some good context around different frameworks and right, like that value selling is, is, is the way to do things, especially today.
Um, cool.
Let's move into some of these interview questions that I've got, but, definitely like, you know, want this to be like conversational as well.

So first question, um, I guess, tell me about in one of these roles that you've had, like how you've positioned, like the product's value to CMO, like a marketing leader or a go to marketing or marketing leader or GTM.
Like in a, in a deal, like help me just kind of better understand that.
Yeah.
You have a great thing at Contentful.
was working with a financial firm actually.
So they're handling billions of dollars, particularly with the CMO and the head of marketing.
I was speaking to both of them.
were kind of both my champions or on a personal level, texting and phone calls back and forth.
But in their situation, they had an old legacy CMS uptime was a huge issue that they were dealing with.
And when it comes to handling money, people want access to it immediately.
They need to be able to trade, to view it, to access it.
Those are all things that are key and very important.

And so how we positioned Contentful was that one, not only are we helping you with this uptime issue that we have, you we went over a lot about our SLA and, you know, 99.9% uptime, you the standard, but we went more in depth and said, hey, think about these other issues and challenges that you may have.
You're a global company.
You're not only working in the U.S.
You're working in 18 different countries in many different languages.
How do we position that value?
It was Contentful as a platform that is built to scale globally.
It's built to continue growing with you and your team.
We have the ability to help you take that to market much faster.

Right now, you have to have all this kind of segmented, these different teams that are working in different languages, you know, in different regions, trying to localize all this different content that can all be centralized into one area where we're reducing your time to market with Contentful, but also providing the safety and security net that you need for that uptime.
And so that was a lot of the value position that we put ourselves in.

You this is a very competitive deal, but I think we brought ourselves to the vendor buy-in because we were able to, one, be the vendor expert and the industry expert in a sense, and they had trust in us.
You know, we had a great team.
You know, obviously, it wasn't all myself.
I worked cross-partnering with my SA, you know, leadership as well, and we showed that we were on board, and this was a partnership.
We actually signed into a multi-year deal in this case, plus some professional services that we, well, that I was able to add on.
Thankfully, bye.
Teaching them, you know, how important it is to build a solid foundation if we're going to be doing this three years, you that we're locking in immediately now.
Yeah.
You know, and so while it took, you know, five months, almost six months to get that done, it was a great deal for me.
It's part of the reason why I was the number one rep in North America that quarter.
And, yeah, it's still one of my favorite deals to work.
It was a lot of work, though.
A lot of partners I had to set up calls with and a lot of back and forth.
A lot of very in-the-weeds questions, I can imagine, in, like, financial services space, you know, buying core infrastructure.
I'm sure procurement was a whole lot of fun.
That's what I'm saying, yeah, particularly, like, the security side of it.
But, no, great story.
I love how you, like, started off with that uptime issue and, like, connected it to why it mattered to their business and then kind of went broad from there, right?
On, like, well, how did you just, like, what was, like, the overall value proposition that you guys were...
just a piece of it.
That was
Delivering, 100%.

In that deal in particular, I guess you touched on this a little bit, but why do you feel like they went with Contentful over one of the top competitors beyond just the fact that you guys conveyed the industry expertise, like you said, and the relationships and anything else that you feel like stood out, either on the Contentful side or just more broad?
Definitely.
lot of it was, you know, of course it was my quarterbacking.
We had relationships because they were moving from what is a traditional CMS.
You you kind of just buy it.
It's kind of a plug and play in a sense.
Headless is a lot different.
It's very technical.
There are a lot of pieces that you need to implement.
You need a hosting solution.
You might need a dam as well.
You know, I work to, well, as a team, work to educate them on that to get them to understand what all is involved in the process, what the implementation looks like.
They were actually under contract at the time.
And when we first began the conversation, they still had a year left.
And so you can imagine you still have to talk them into actually making the purchase now rather than waiting until that contract ends.
And so a lot of that was, like I said, goes into the education of, hey, it's going to take this much time to get to building it out and to actually implementing it and then going live.

So like I said, it's not just them being confident in that we were an institution expert, but we provided all the resources they need to be confident that they could actually get it done within the timeframe that they had set for themselves.
While also providing them all the contacts that they needed to be able to speak to, like I said, myself and the head of marketing, you know, we were on a texting basis, phone calls back and forth.

You know, I was personally setting up a lot of these calls with the partners that they needed so that we could get still done on time rather than have it stretch out where they were going for all these different evaluations.

I kind of put it all together and, you know, I know everyone uses these days, but, you know, mutual success plan, mutual action plan, you know, just make sure that we're aligned on what that timeline looks like.
And I think a lot of that is the reason why they.

Decided to go with Contentful, is that not only did we show our expertise, but we kind of held their hand to the process as well as someone who wasn't well-versed in how to establish, you know, headless CMS in their environment.
Really impressive.
It sounds like you're, like, not afraid to get into the technical weeds.
Do you enjoy, like, diving deeper into, like, the product and, you know, the things that sometimes, like, AEs just sort of punt to the solutions engineers, solutions architects?
It's big for me.

I've always been a technical person, you know, it's, I've been working with computers and doing things since I was, you know, a teenager, so understanding technical piece is big, you know, I've tried my hand in coding as well.
I don't enjoy all the process, but, you know, I like to understand how things work, so that's important to me.
That's awesome.
And then one more follow-up, like, what metrics or outcomes for that deal, like, did you connect to, into
Contentful and, you know, what the company, what the client was ultimately trying to accomplish.
Like what kind of KPIs or what kind of outcomes were you talking about?
Well, yeah, one, I was mentioning the time to market.
So before they had a lot of segmented teams because they were working in different regions and different countries, different languages.
They were able to cut that time down.
For example, if something normally took two months to get it out to every single translation in every region, we could cut that down to two weeks.
Due to the fact that every team works within Contentful and they have their own separate areas where they can handle their translations, they can do their localization as well.
A good example of that is Spain, Spanish is a bit different than let's say Central or South America, Spanish.
So you need to be able to localize that and make those small changes.
And so that was a big part of it for us.
The uptime, not so much.
I mean, that's pretty standard.
You know, it's in our agreement.
So that's something you just show and say, hey, this is 99.99% uptime.
So we wanted to focus more on the big picture.
of what we could talk about then, as well as handling the content that they had when it came to financial data, how it was possible if they would like to bring that into potential.
It's actually a project that we saved for another time, so potential expansion.
Unfortunately, I moved to the Southeast Territory after that, so that was unfortunate.
Yeah, yeah, the time to value, and then what was the connection to revenue on that deal, would you say?
Well, the biggest thing is this company managed, I think it was around $600 billion.
They were moving in there throughout their market, so we're approaching the trillion mark.
And the idea is that, one, with uptime, losing a single extra minute, 10 minutes a day throughout the year, how many billions of dollars could that be?
So I think that was the easiest connection to make, and from there was how they could grow their revenue instead.
We were talking about, by now, moving all this into these different markets, and as you continue to span to additional languages and additional countries, as you localize and translate.
How much additional revenue are you bringing in by being able to manage all the content that you have and bring in all these additional customers?
For sure.
Yeah.
A better like digital experience, being able to be more flexible with the different regions that they serve.
And the biggest thing is also it's not just websites.
Contentful goes through all digital channels.
So thinking about if they were to establish a mobile app, how they could use the same content through Contentful and push it to these different digital channels.
Once again, for all these different markets as well.
So a lot of it is a time to market and, you know, making it an easier customer experience as well.
And something that's very seamless and very familiar as well.
I love it.
I love it.
You know, Contentful very, very well, which is good.
I mean, we're in the content business ourselves here, as you know.
Let's switch gears in the sales process a little bit.
So talk to me about like an outreach experiment or campaign.
Pain that you helped, you know, set up or that you executed on either from like your BDR days or if you had to do outbound as an AE, that's fine too.
But just tell me like a story about one that helped drive a lot of, you know, new pipeline.
Yeah, this is actually quite easy for me, but I'll use a recent one.
At Cypress, actually, everything was pretty much inbound before I joined.
They had downsized the team a lot.
They only had a few AEs and so they were just working inbound deals.
That's kind of why I was brought in.
I had experience doing outbound.
A new product was released, accessibility.
so Cypress is in the automated testing realm.
You know, it allows engineers and developers to integrate with their CICD pipeline and test a floor of applications, make sure it's working correctly.
And so with the introduction of accessibility, we needed to figure out a way to push this product towards not just existing customers, but also new customers.
And so I built a lot of that outbound sequence by actually working with the SC who was very strong with accessibility inside Cypress.
And so thought it was lot of the secret.
Once I would send it over and say, hey, take a look at this.
Does this make sense?
If I send this to you, would you be interested?
So we kind of went back and forth on the iterations and making some changes and slight things to it.
I think the biggest change I made was personalizing some of that that went out to the potential customers and the existing ones.
The biggest thing being accessibility statements are something that every company has.
Why are we not utilizing this in our messaging?
Finding ways to say, hey, this is clearly important to you and your company.
I see that you have it right here on your site or maybe you mentioned a press release or blog post.
Let me tell you why Cypress makes sense here and why we should have this conversation and ensure that you are accessible.
And also linking that to a campaign that we had in Europe as well.
One thing I noticed is that they had the EAA, European Accessibility Act, that was due.
It was pretty much mandatory to have something in place for accessibility by June of this year.
And so I harped on that to bring that back into that campaign and say, hey, this is something that we can help you with.
We can make sure that you're with.
Then regulations that you don't have to deal with any audits in the future.
When it comes to accessibility, you have a solution in place.
And just finding ways to get creative and to make sure that it's attention-rabbing and that it makes sense and it brings value for those who would be interested.
And one thing I will say is for me personally, when I first started the campaign, I was able to flip forward meetings within the first couple of weeks, which was awesome.
But I also extended it to my team as well.
And as a team, we built 500,000 in pipeline in just a month.
That is a great story.
My first sales job, I started at PwC Consulting, but I went to Rainforest QA.
Have you heard of Rainforest before?
Yes, that's an old one.
Yeah.
And it's very hard.
mean, the story resonates because it's very hard to generate pipeline outbound with developers, right?
They're the last people who want to talk to sales.
So you have to get more technical.
And your story did a great job of highlighting that.
Yeah.
Yeah.
They're not going to take a meeting based on a gift card or anything like that.
Yeah, you have to get creative.

And that's been the big thing, I think, for me these past few years is just finding other ways to be creative, something that, you know, kind of is different than what they typically see in their inbox.
For sure.
So were you, since you were a contract at Cypress, like, were you working differently than, like, other AEs at the company?
Or did everybody have, like, a responsibility to do outbound?
Like, just help me kind of understand your role a little bit better there among the team.
At first, no, I wouldn't say that they had responsibility for outbound.
Like I said, everything was pretty much inbound.
It was probably around that time when I built that initial sequence for the accessibility product that they started to push to more, I guess you could say, you know, sales-led growth.
think that's, like, the buzzword these days.
So a little more outbound was being done, but I was still pretty much the one building sequences and sharing that with the team.
It's just something I kind of enjoyed doing probably on my BBR days, I guess.
And yeah, I feel like I'm pretty good at it.
But I know I had typical AE responsibilities.
It's just the manager that brought me on, he was really excited about the fact that I had outbound experience.
And that was something that he hoped I could bring, as well as a little more general sales experience.
I guess you could say I had a little more history with sales in general, being an AE.
And some of my sales, I guess, methodologies were kind of similar to his.
And pushing more, trying to increase the ACV, for example.
It makes a lot of sense.
This is just personally curious.
What tools did you use either to execute the sequence or to create the copy?
Yeah, so Outreach, which I'm super familiar with.
Pretty much everyone uses it.
And that was for all the sequences.
Growthx customer.
Yeah, they make things pretty easy.
So I do like Outreach.
But a lot of the research was.
Went into using ChatGPT for one.
It's been super helpful.
I also use Claude as well.
It's very good for kind of cutting down the time that I need to make changes to emails that I want to send out.
You usually I'll draft an email and kind of run it through there and kind of think of ways where I can make changes to certain sentences or, you know, ways that are more attention-driving.
So that's helpful as well.
Also, I don't quite remember the name of it because I just happened to find it, but it's based off of ChatGPT.
It was a for me to input an Excel sheet based on the accounts.
Actually search the websites and find the exact accessibility statements to see whether certain companies have them or not.
And that made it easier to figure out what I should target first and kind of tier those accounts.
And, of course, LinkedIn now, the usual stuff is available.
For sure.
For sure.
It's really interesting about, like, uploading the spreadsheet and, you know, being able to have that be a part of how you're targeting or how you're creating copy.
Both.
Both.
So you would think creating copy because to me, the accessibility statements were important.
Oh, you would like bring the exact statement?
actual statement.
say, hey, I noticed you have this exact statement, whether it was in a press release, on your website, wherever it may be.
Say, hey, I noticed this is your statement.
Let's have a conversation.
This is why we should have a conversation.
And so that was super helpful and it generated a lot of conversations.
So it could, and sorry, I'm digging in so much.
It's just very interesting.
Like you could essentially like programmatically add those statements to the different emails you were reaching out to based on the account.
And I wish I could have done it programmatically.
I had to do it manually, but I did pull all the statements, you know, using AI, of course.
And that way I didn't have to do it all myself.
You know, I just plugged it in, said, hey, search for those statements.
These are the websites.
Because the biggest thing with chat GPT, it can't really suggest an update.
Yeah.
Which is kind of an issue.
And so.
So I had to find that other site and it was able to go through Excel sheets and actually search those specific sites and then find whether they had accessibility statements.
then from there, so you see, was a process of trying to do what I wanted.
Yeah, I mean, it's tough though.

Like most times in sales, you get so busy just like working your deals that it's hard to think strategically like that and then execute on an idea or a strategy that's going to take like daily moving the needle.
And this is like such a good example of you were working these deals, but you were also thinking more bigger picture, right?
Like what is going to help me work smarter up front so I don't have to work as hard, right?
Throughout, you found like this hypothesis around what would resonate and then you took the time to actually step-by-step build a machine that could execute.
And it was, you know, to your point, it's to your point, it's still manual, but like.
Yeah, but you cut down.
A lot of the extra, you know, tedious, you know, kind of monotonous work, you know, it's a bit easier if you can just run it through an AI program.

Yeah, and I mean, if it's just automated in AI, like, it won't be that differentiated, you know, like, part of what we do here at GrowthX is, as you know, right, like, we build these really sophisticated AI workflows, but there's humans in the loop for all of that.

And we take a lot of pride in, like, making sure that, like, we've read what is generated and made some of those human touch refinements from SEO experts and people who have writing backgrounds before a client is reviewing it.
So it's important to have that blend.
Okay, I got one more kind of longer question, a little, you know, question by question.
And then I would love to hear what questions you have for me.
So, love the story about the financial services firm.
Tell me, like, about another larger deal that you closed or more strategic deal that you closed.
And just, like, walk me through, you know, that story and ultimately, like, how you closed it.
Sure.
I'm thinking about which one I think would be better.
I have media industry and telecommunications, which sounds more interesting to you.
Man, media sounds more interesting to me.
Okay, media.
But whichever one you'd prefer is fine with No, we can go media.
So in this particular case, the initial discovery began with the SVP of digital transformation and IT, as well as the head of engineering and his team.
So we've kind got both here.
I actually started off on the wrong foot in this particular call.
They came in expecting a demo.
We came in with questions.
We wanted to better understand what the use case was going to be, what the challenges were.
So it already started bad.
And this was a customer from the Northeast, a little more straightforward, I should say.
There you go.
And so- No Southern hospitality, huh?
Yeah.
Exactly.
I had to overcome that.
That was the initial challenge.
And from there, was just compromising and say, I understand.
We had a misunderstanding here.
What I want to do is set aside that hour so that we can demo for you and the engineering team.
But this is what I need from you.
I just want to ensure that we can personalize this demo.
We can set it up in a way that it makes sense for you and your team and that we're hitting home on all the points that are important to you.
And so I was able to gather that information.
Thankfully, we were able to set a time for the demo and circle back next week.
Brought them on.
I'm going see I'm working with my SE at this time, my sleeper's engineer.

And this particular media company, I don't know if it's giving too much away, but they are one of the oldest publications in the history, I guess you could say, one of the longest running publications.
And so they have a lot of content that they're dealing with, almost 200 years worth.
And so in this particular case, we had to figure out the right story to tell.
We didn't actually have a media company that was similar to this customer that would be the biggest logo in this particular sense that we'd be bringing on.
So we did a lot of work and working with other kind of similar companies that did a lot of posts with content, you a lot of blog content, some of them were like video game content, as an example.

But something that was similar when it came to scale and really telling the story and getting them on board and understanding that we're enterprise ready and that we can handle the amount of content that they have.
And that was what went into the demo and our presentation when it came to hitting home on some of their points and how they had a lot of different channels that they were working through.
Not just the website, but they had podcasts, they had videos, they did a lot through social media as well.
One thing I even mentioned was the fact that they could consider other things like how they produce content digitally through, let's say, smartwatches.
So kind of putting ideas in their head of how they could use Contentful.
And so the demo process went very well.
From there, we were able to move on to figuring out how we could speak to the actual content team.
Those were actually going to be writing, creating the content, editing it, making changes to it.
How we could set that potential call up.
And.
here's where we hit another roadblock.
They weren't really on board yet with bringing us on to give that demo to them.
We had to have a few more conversations on the actual implementation and what that looked like.
So in this case, we actually couldn't agree on what that looked like.

And we brought in a solutions architect, a little different from our solutions engineer, a little more technical, a little more in-depth with the implementation, you how the data traverses through Contentful.

And so that required another conversation with their engineering team and their development team, kind of going back and forth on what that would look like and giving them the confidence that Contentful could handle the way they wanted to set it up.
And from there, you know, the SVP is kind of overall of this project.
He's overseeing everything.
Me and him are able to build a great relationship.
To the point similar to that previous story I told you, we were on a texting basis and phone calls, which was great.
And as we move through this, security was the next thing that came up.
And so security assessments was something we had to quickly handle.
I would say that wasn't much of a challenge.
And then finally, after overcoming these three different things, we were able to bring in the content.
you.
Thank

We were actually able to have that conversation, produce the demo, and say, hey, we want to learn more about your workflows and see how they align and give you an idea of what your data is going to look like utilizing the contentful platform.
And so with that writing team, that content team, we were able to win them and bring them on board.
And now legal was the next issue we ran into.
A bit of a legal back and forth that took place for three to four weeks, a lot of red lines from our legal team, their legal team.
It came to a point where I kind of had to head the situation and say, hey, let's hop on a call.
Let's see if we can hash this out.
We have a deadline that we've set.
